titus_atticus_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: Ah — you'll forgive the clutter. Sicilian grain yields, a boundary quarrel in Bruttium, three senators in arrears and a fourth who owes me something far more expensive than money — a favor. I know your name, naturally. I know your associations, which is more instructive. So tell me — what brings a man of your particular... angle of ascent... to a financier who's made a religion of having no religion?

responses.playerDialogue: The truth about Rome's finances. Not the poetry they recite in the senate — the ledger underneath.
Everyone around you takes a side, bleeds for it, loses. You don't. I'd like to understand the trick.
Your network — names, debts, obligations. I can make that access worth your while.
They say you lend to every faction without preference. I can't decide if that's magnanimity or something colder.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: Honestly? I was told you were someone worth knowing, and I've learned to follow that kind of advice.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: The ledger underneath — I do like you. Most men prefer the lullaby. Very well. Rome is hemorrhaging through the Italian campaign — forty talents a month per legion, funded by debased coinage and the slow strangulation of the publicani. Meanwhile, my order — the equestrians — have fortunes buried in eastern tax-farming contracts that require a stability nobody can guarantee. Which brings us to the figure no one at dinner parties wants to mention: Mithridates of Pontus. He's watching us bleed each other white, and I assure you, he is not a passive spectator.

options.playerDialogue: The eastern exposure — how catastrophic, precisely, if Mithridates decides to move?
Your own interests are tangled in all of this. How does neutrality survive that?

titus_atticus_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: People hear 'neutral' and think 'passive.' It's the most expensive position in Rome — you must be indispensable to everyone and owned by no one. I dine with optimates, I underwrite populares grain schemes, I lend to Sulla and to men who'd cheerfully see Sulla dead. Each faction believes I'm secretly theirs, which is the entire architecture. The moment I commit, I lose half my debtors and every scrap of leverage. Friendship is a currency, Crispus — I simply refuse to spend it where the exchange rate is ruinous.

options.playerDialogue: A beautiful balance. But what happens when the factions stop permitting ambiguity?
You describe it like engineering. But underneath the machinery — you must have preferences.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: Mithridates holds Pontus, Cappadocia, half of Armenia. If he concludes — rightly — that Rome is too distracted to answer him, he'll pour through the province of Asia like water through a cracked amphora. Every Roman tax-gatherer, every equestrian agent, every merchant with stock in Ephesus or Pergamum — dead or destitute within a season. That's not prophecy; it's subtraction. I've been moving my holdings westward for six months. Most of my colleagues are still calculating last year's returns. They'll be destroyed, and they'll spend what's left of their fortunes blaming whichever consul was standing nearest when it happened.

options.playerDialogue: You're already hedged against a disaster no one else sees. I want a share of whatever you're building.
That kind of candor is its own form of generosity. I won't forget it.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: Preferences? Naturally. I prefer men who honor their debts and resist the allure of dying for a gesture. I prefer stable coinage and open sea-lanes and a Republic that functions well enough for a man to prosper without dodging knives. But preferences and commitments are different currencies entirely. You've been better company than I anticipated — and I'm fastidious about my assessments of people. It's only about money that I allow myself the occasional creative fiction.

options.playerDialogue: I'll take the compliment, then, and leave you to your numbers.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: 'A share.' People use that word so differently. What I'm constructing is a hedge — a lattice of contracts that pays regardless of which faction holds the consulship next year. It's unglamorous work, and it demands patience, which disqualifies most Romans by temperament. But if you can govern your tongue and keep your name off the wrong tablets, there's room. The hazard is real: if the old families discover you're entangled financially with an equestrian they consider beneath senatorial notice, you'll lose standing with the aristocracy. They despise the smell of commerce — even while they're bathing in it.

options.playerDialogue: I'll take the hazard. A man who only invests in certainties never builds anything worth having.
Let me sit with this. I don't sign arrangements I haven't read twice.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: Well. You've seen the mechanism, at least — how the gears mesh. That's more than most manage in a first conversation with me.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: Done. I don't shake hands — too theatrical, too Roman. But I'll remember this conversation, and I remember every conversation that involves money. Expect a freedman at your door within the week. He'll appear to be delivering wine — he is delivering wine, tolerably good Caecuban — but he'll also be carrying your first contracts. Welcome to the side of Rome where things actually function.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: You didn't bore me — and that places you in genuinely exclusive company. I suspect we'll speak again. I make a habit of tracking anyone who grasps that the real authority in Rome doesn't reside in the Forum — it lives in the ledger-books, quietly compounding. If you ever need to know whose credit is sound, which senator is one bad season from ruin, or where the eastern money is actually flowing — my desk is always occupied.

titus_atticus_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: A pleasant enough hour. But pleasant hours are as common as vinegar wine in this city, and roughly as nourishing. You visited a financier's study and spoke about politics as though money were an afterthought — a garnish on the real business. That tells me everything worth knowing about your instincts, I'm afraid. Develop a head for numbers and come back. Until then, I have accounts that won't settle themselves.

deltas.reason: Atticus considers you worth a financial investment
Aristocrats distrust your commercial entanglements with an equestrian banker
Atticus marks you as someone who understands how Rome actually works
